2mo ago ORIGINAL: 'PS leaders live at the intersection of customer outcomes and PS margin. Treating this as a tradeoff is the wrong frame. The real mandate is to design decisions that deliver both.' — framed PS as a strategic growth engine, not a cost center. 52+ reactions.

5mo ago REPOST: AI in PS podcast — 'AI is everywhere until you check the numbers.'

You've written about this recently: proving value is the hardest part of any modernization effort. Without clear measurement, the benefits feel abstract.

For intelligent delivery models in CS and PS, the metrics that matter are usually: time-to-value per customer onboarding, configuration completion rates, rework rate on implementation environments, and consultant utilization against delivery tasks vs. administrative setup. BMC's AI story is compelling — Control-M AI capabilities, Helix agentic AI, BMC AMI intelligence. The question that determines whether that story becomes revenue is: how fast can your PS org get customers productive on those capabilities?

Enterprise PS organizations that can't adopt their own company's AI features quickly lose the credibility to sell them. The gap between product readiness and PS readiness is where AI investment stalls.

Beacon is the orchestration layer that closes that gap — so your PS and CS teams can deliver on the AI vision as fast as your product teams build it. Happy to speak for 15 minutes.

Leading delivery across India, LATAM, and EMEA simultaneously is a different problem than managing a regional practice. The hardest part isn't hiring (you're clearly doing that). It's getting hundreds of consultants to deliver to the same quality bar when they're on different continents, trained differently, and working on implementations that vary wildly in complexity.

Beacon helps PS leaders build a delivery playbook that scales across regions, not just headcount.

The Anthology SIS/ERP absorption didn't just add clients — it added a delivery multiplier with no ramp time.

You came from an environment where delivery complexity was manageable through process engineering. Higher-ed is different: every campus has a different ERP footprint, different IT maturity, and no two implementations follow the same path.

Beacon works with PS leaders at companies scaling through acquisition. We compress time-to-go-live without proportional headcount growth — by standardizing the configuration and handoff layers that eat the most time.

Worth 20 minutes to see if it maps to what you're solving?

— [Your name] | Use as first touch within the first 3 months of Denny's tenure. References the Anthology acquisition and his Microsoft background without being presumptuous. | The scale gap in higher-ed PS delivery | Denny,

Here's the tension most VP PS leaders face in higher-ed SaaS right now: the client base is growing (through organic growth or acquisition), but implementation capacity doesn't scale linearly.

At Microsoft, you scaled support through process discipline and tooling. The same lever exists in PS delivery — but it requires automating the configuration and scoping layers that most teams still do manually.

Ellucian's PS org is at an inflection point. Beacon helps teams like yours scale delivery output without scaling headcount at the same rate.

(4) 5 years + 10x regional growth: he has built the delivery machine and now needs to systematise it. (5) 'Practical applications of neuro-symbolic AI' language: he's implementing AI in delivery, not just talking about it. | Scale angle — he delivered 24 go-lives in one quarter and received an award for it. The implicit question: how do you make 24 go-lives systematically repeatable rather than heroic? Beacon answers this. | His 3-month-old post: 'Q4 Business Milestone Award — 24 successful go-lives within a single project.' This is extraordinary delivery velocity. Open with: 'Two back-to-back Business Milestone Awards in 2025 — and 24 go-lives in Q4 alone — is a delivery pace that most enterprise SaaS orgs would consider impossible. The question I'd love to explore: what does the infrastructure behind that look like, and where does it still rely on heroic individual effort?' | HIGH | PRIORITY 1 — 5-year veteran, 10x growth, 24 go-lives, AI cert, very active. Ideal entry point for EMEA. | 24 go-lives in Q4 is extraordinary. What breaks at that pace? | Dimitris,

Two Business Milestone Awards in 2025 — and 24 go-lives in a single Q4 project. That's not a delivery team, that's a delivery machine.

The question we ask leaders who've operated at that intensity: which parts of the process still depend on your best people being on every engagement? Configuration, workflow setup, environment orchestration — most orgs at your scale still do these manually.

Beacon automates that layer. The delivery quality you've built through people and culture, we make systematic through AI.

Would you have 20 minutes to pressure-test the idea against your Q4 model? | Use within 2 weeks of his award/milestone posts. | You scaled EMEA 10x. The next 10x needs a different infrastructure. | Dimitris,

Five years, 10x regional growth, 24 go-lives in Q4. You've proven you can build a delivery org that scales.

The infrastructure behind the first 10x was people, culture, and high-agency teams — exactly what you described in your anniversary post. The infrastructure for the next 10x needs to be systematic: AI-driven configuration, delivery pattern libraries, automated workflow setup.

Beacon is that infrastructure layer. Leaders who've built delivery orgs from scratch are the fastest to see the value. 20 minutes? | Use referencing his 5-year anniversary post; works well for relationship-building conversations. | Your 5-year reflection said 'push AI deeper into delivery.' Here's the push. | Dimitris,

Your 5-year post said it clearly: 'now pushing AI deeper into how we operate, decide, and support our clients.' Your Google Cloud GenAI cert shows you're serious about it.

Most delivery leaders stop at AI for planning. Beacon applies AI to the delivery workflow itself — configuration, workflow setup, environment orchestration. It's the operational layer underneath the planning platform.

You've built the high-agency delivery culture. We've built the AI infrastructure that makes it scalable. Worth 20 minutes? | Use referencing his 5-year anniversary post and GenAI cert — connects personal ambition to Beacon's offering. |  |  |

Worth 20 minutes?

Chris | Use when he's in growth mode or has just announced a new product/acquisition. | The platform paradox: more capabilities, slower customer time-to-value | Hi Chris,

Datapeople. Pearl Meyer. Korn Ferry. Each partnership makes Payscale more powerful — and more complex to implement for new customers.

That's the platform paradox: the more comprehensive your solution, the longer it takes customers to go live, and the higher the churn risk in year one.

Beacon compresses implementation time by automating the configuration-heavy steps. Customers go live faster, adoption is higher, and your CS team spends less time on setup.

Open to a conversation?

Chris | Use after a new product launch, partnership, or acquisition announcement. | From ZoomInfo COO to Payscale CEO — the implementation challenge changes | Hi Chris,

At ZoomInfo, implementation was a relatively constrained problem — the product was deep but narrow. At Payscale, you're connecting compensation, TA, benchmarking, and executive pay — and customers need all of it configured correctly before they get value.

That's a fundamentally different implementation challenge. Most tooling isn't built for it.

Beacon was. It automates the orchestration layer of complex, multi-module implementations — so your team can scale enterprise onboarding without scaling headcount proportionally.

Post 1 — 7 hours ago [ORIGINAL]: Published article in PropertyCasualty360: "Stopping system-hopping: Underwriting speed is a design problem." SIGNAL: She is personally wrestling with delivery complexity and system fragmentation — this is her public positioning as CCO.

Post 2 — 2 weeks ago [ORIGINAL]: Article on "one source of truth" as the foundation of modern data strategy in insurance. Argues fragmented data kills AI potential. SIGNAL: Unified delivery data/standardisation is top of mind.

Post 3 — 2 weeks ago [PERSONAL]: International Women's Day post; her daughter is her biggest inspiration. SIGNAL: Family-motivated, emotionally open — not just corporate. Warm personal angle possible.
